Document:
# Architecture & Design
OpenEdge 14 is built on several core architectural patterns that determine how the game works. Understanding these patterns is crucial for effective development.
## The Big Picture
[Robust Toolbox - .NET Engine] | [Entity-Component-System (ECS)] | [IoC Container - Dependency Injection] | [Event-Driven Communication] | [Network Synchronization Layer] | [OpenEdge 14 Systems & Gameplay]
## Five Core Architectural Concepts
1. [textlink="Entity-Component-System (ECS)" link="OE14_DEV_Arch_ECS"]
[bold]What it is:[/bold] A design pattern where the world is made up of [bold]Entities[/bold] that have [bold]Components[/bold], and [bold]Systems[/bold] that process those components.
[bold]Why we use it:[/bold] - Flexible composition — combine components to create any type of object - Efficient processing — systems only iterate over relevant entities - Easy to test — systems are pure logic, easily mockable - Supports networking — each component can be networked independently
[bold]Example:[/bold] - Entity: A player character - Components: HealthComponent, InventoryComponent, TransformComponent, CharacterStatsComponent - System: HealthSystem processes damage from HealthComponent
2. [textlink="IoC Container" link="OE14_DEV_Arch_IoC"]
[bold]What it is:[/bold] A registry of [bold]services[/bold] (systems, managers, utilities) that can be accessed globally via [mono]IoCManager.Resolve\()[/mono].
[bold]Why we use it:[/bold] - Decoupled code — components don't need to know about concrete implementations - Dependency injection — services are provided, not created - Easy mocking for tests - Prevents tight coupling
[bold]Example:[/bold]
var entityManager = IoCManager.Resolve(); var actionSystem = IoCManager.Resolve();
3. [textlink="Network Synchronization" link="OE14_DEV_Arch_Netcode"]
[bold]What it is:[/bold] A system that automatically keeps server and client in sync by transmitting component changes.
[bold]Why we use it:[/bold] - Multiplayer support — all players see the same world - Server authority — server is always correct, client interpolates - Bandwidth efficient — only changed data is transmitted - Transparent — marked with the AutoNetworkedField attribute
[bold]Example:[/bold]
[DataField, AutoNetworkedField] public int Health { get; set; } // Syncs to client automatically
4. [textlink="Event System" link="OE14_DEV_Arch_Events"]
[bold]What it is:[/bold] A publish-subscribe system where systems emit events, and other systems listen and react.
[bold]Why we use it:[/bold] - Loose coupling — systems don't call each other directly - Flexibility — new reactions can be added without changing core logic - Composability — complex behaviors from simple event chains - Clear intent — what's happening is obvious from events
[bold]Example:[/bold] - Event: DamageEvent (entity took X damage) - Listeners: HealthSystem (apply damage), CombatLogSystem (log the hit), StatusEffectSystem (apply bleeding)
5. [textlink="Modifier System" link="OE14_DEV_Arch_Modifiers"]
[bold]What it is:[/bold] A system for applying temporary stat changes (bonuses) from various sources without mutating base values.
[bold]Why we use it:[/bold] - Composability — multiple sources can add bonuses - Tracking — know which source each bonus came from - Overflow protection — handled when modifiers exceed limits - Reusability — same system for spells, items, buffs, etc.
[bold]Example:[/bold] - Base Stat: INT = 8 - Modifiers: +2 from Metamagic spell, +1 from item bonus - Effective: INT = 11 (capped at 10, overflow = 1) - Display: "INT: 8 -> 10 (+3 Spell, overflow: 1)"
## The Three Layers
### Layer 1: Content Layer
[bold]What goes here:[/bold] YAML prototypes, item definitions, spell definitions, job definitions
[bold]Located:[/bold] [mono]Resources/Prototypes/_OE14/[/mono]
[bold]Change frequency:[/bold] Very often (balancing, new items, etc.)
[bold]Example:[/bold]
- type: entity id: IronSword components: - type: MeleeWeapon damage: 25
### Layer 2: System Layer
[bold]What goes here:[/bold] C# systems that process game logic
[bold]Located:[/bold] [mono]Content.Server/_OE14/Systems/[/mono] and [mono]Content.Shared/_OE14/[/mono]
[bold]Change frequency:[/bold] Moderately (adding features, fixing bugs)
[bold]Example:[/bold]
public class CombatSystem : EntitySystem { public override void Update(float frameTime) { // Process combat events } }
### Layer 3: Framework Layer
[bold]What goes here:[/bold] RobustToolbox engine code (don't modify!)
[bold]Located:[/bold] [mono]RobustToolbox/[/mono] subdirectory
[bold]Change frequency:[/bold] Never (we use it as-is)
[bold]Example:[/bold] Entity manager, component registration, network serialization
## Data Flow: How the Game Works
### A Simple Example: Attacking an Enemy
1. Player clicks enemy (Input) | 2. ActionBlockerSystem checks if action is allowed (can player act?) | 3. CombatSystem processes attack | 4. Calculates damage: base damage + STR bonus - armor | 5. Creates DamageEvent with damage amount | 6. HealthSystem listens to DamageEvent | 7. Applies damage to enemy HealthComponent | 8. HealthComponent value changes (Health -= damage) | 9. AutoNetworkedField syncs new Health value to client | 10. Client receives update, displays new health bar | 11. If health <= 0: - DeathEvent emitted - PhysicsSystem disables movement - AnimationSystem plays death animation - LootSystem creates dropped items
Each step is independent and uses events to communicate.
## Key Terminology
- [bold]Entity[/bold] — An object in the world (player, item, NPC, effect) - [bold]Component[/bold] — A data container attached to an entity (health, position, inventory) - [bold]System[/bold] — Logic that processes entities with certain components - [bold]Service[/bold] — A global manager (EntityManager, TransformSystem, etc.) - [bold]Event[/bold] — A message sent when something happens (damage, death, pickup) - [bold]Modifier[/bold] — A temporary stat bonus from spells, items, or effects - [bold]Network[/bold] — Syncing state between server and client - [bold]Prototype[/bold] — YAML definition of an entity or item
## Important Design Principles
### Server Authority The server is always right. Clients can predict and interpolate, but server values are canonical. Never trust client input for critical decisions.
### Network Efficiency Only sync changes. Don't re-send data that hasn't changed. Batch updates when possible.
### No Magic Numbers Use named enums and constants. A health value of 50 should be a named constant.
### Separation of Concerns Systems should do one thing well. Don't mix combat logic with UI code.
### Composition Over Inheritance Use components to add behavior, not class hierarchies. Much more flexible.
## Next Steps
Choose a concept to dive deeper into:
- [textlink="Read ECS System" link="OE14_DEV_Arch_ECS"] — understanding entity/component/system - [textlink="Read IoC Container" link="OE14_DEV_Arch_IoC"] — resolving services - [textlink="Read Network Sync" link="OE14_DEV_Arch_Netcode"] — syncing with client - [textlink="Read Event System" link="OE14_DEV_Arch_Events"] — creating custom logic - [textlink="Read Modifier System" link="OE14_DEV_Arch_Modifiers"] — adding stat bonuses
Or jump to [textlink="System Reference" link="OE14_DEV_SystemReference"] for a specific system.
